input_semantic_node: Abortion is morally justifiable (context: moral personhood from conception)
central_semantic_node: The Justifiability of Termination vs. Fetal Personhood Axioms
argument_reasons: The input presents a nested contextual loop where the justification of abortion is tethered to the ontological status of the fetus as a person with adult-equivalent rights.
Axiomatic Conflict: The argument assumes a 'personhood at conception' premise which traditionally functions as a defeater for 'moral justification' in deontological frameworks.

ontological_signature.function: To determine the hierarchy of rights between two entities within a biological-dependent relationship.
ontological_signature.form: Recursive self-referential loop (Personhood nested in Personhood).
ontological_signature.tension: The tension between the right to bodily autonomy and the right to life of a dependent person-entity.
ontological_signature.limit: Dependent on the axiomatic definition of 'Person', which is analytically uncollapsable without a meta-ethical anchor.

conceptual_disintegration.definition: The ending of a pregnancy so it does not result in a birth.
When an action is considered 'right' or 'okay' because it follows a good rule or produces a fair result.
The status of being a member of the group that has the highest level of protection and rights.
The very first moment when a potential life starts.

preconceptual_transduction: Imagine two people who need to share the same life-support suit. If one person owns the suit but the other person was put inside it without asking, is it okay for the owner to open the suit if it means the other person can't breathe? It depends on if the second person is seen as a 'real' person with a right to stay, or just a guest.

iterations.base_statement: A fetus is a person from conception
iterations.context: If personhood is tied to DNA/Conception, the moral landscape freezes; if it is tied to consciousness, it remains fluid.

global_evaluation.criteria: The argument rests on a circular contextual nesting of 'personhood' which is an unproven axiom, leading to a logical stalemate between competing rights.

deductive_observations.origin: If a fetus has the same moral rights as an adult (P), and adults have a right to life (Q).
deductive_observations.conclusion: The fetus has a right to life equal to an adult.
deductive_observations.notes: Direct implication of the provided context.

subjectivities: The definition of when 'personhood' begins is a subjective metaphysical assignment, not an empirical discovery.
The weight given to bodily autonomy versus the right to life varies by moral framework (Utilitarian vs Kantian).

counterexamples.refuted_statement: If a fetus is a person, abortion is never justifiable.
counterexamples.description: Judith Jarvis Thomson's Violinist analogy: Being a person with a right to life does not include the right to use another person's body against their will.
counterexamples.notes: Challenges the 'automatic' transition from personhood to non-justifiability.

inductive_observations.observed_pattern: Legal systems globally fluctuate on this, often linking rights to viability or birth.
inductive_observations.inference: Societal intelligence does not recognize 'personhood at conception' as a universal objective truth.

preconceptual_conclusion: We can't agree yet because we haven't decided if the tiny seed is a 'person' or a 'promise of a person'.
emerging_theory_or_intuition: The 'Right to Life' (RTL) and 'Right to Autonomy' (RTA) are in a field of computational irreducibility because they are both Max-Priority Axioms; the system only collapses when one is arbitrarily demoted.

rows: Depends on the hierarchy of rights which is not fixed in the prompt.
A fetus is a person from conception
A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
Derived from the undefined personhood status.
Autonomy permits the removal of a dependent entity
Conflict between self-ownership and duty to another person-entity.

formula_dictionary.P: A fetus is a person from conception
formula_dictionary.R: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
formula_dictionary.B: Bodily autonomy is an absolute right

why_the_formula_structure_is_incorrect: The current structure does not account for the fact that P (Personhood) is often treated as a logical 'Not' for A (Justifiability) in the specific provided context.
boolean_formula_to_natural_language: If a fetus is a person (P) and bodily autonomy is not an absolute right (!B), then that implies abortion is false (not justifiable); otherwise, it is true.
conclusion: If personhood is assumed at conception, the justification of abortion requires the elevation of bodily autonomy over the right to life, but because personhood itself is a recursive metaphysical claim rather than a biological fact, the justification remains a suspended state of normative preference. And for all this, the inevitability remains that the moral status of abortion is a byproduct of how a society chooses to define the boundaries of its moral community.

collapse_implications.statement: A fetus is a person from conception
collapse_implications.implication_by_false_state: Abortion becomes a low-stakes medical procedure similar to tissue removal.
collapse_implications.implication_by_true_state: Abortion becomes a high-stakes conflict of interest between two equal rights-bearers.

logical_tension.paradox: The fetus is a 'person' only through the biological mediation of the mother, yet the 'personhood' is used to deny the mother's mediation.
logical_tension.ambiguity: Defining 'rights' without defining the 'capacity' to exercise them.
logical_tension.useful_contradiction: The fetus is simultaneously 'part of' and 'other than' the host.

analogous_reorganization: The Sovereign State vs. The Dependent Enclave
A program running within an OS that threatens to crash the OS
implications: If personhood is at conception, the state must technically treat every miscarriage as a potential investigation.
If autonomy is absolute, no person has a 'right' to the body of another, regardless of their personhood status.

global_reevaluation.criteria: The input loops personhood definitions within personhood contexts, creating an infinite epistemic recursion that cannot be solved without an external axiomatic 'cut'.

reconclusion: The moral justifiability of abortion is logically contingent upon the 'Personhood Axiom'; if P is true, A is only true if Autonomy > Life; if P is false, A is true by default. Since the prompt nests P as a given context, it creates a structural lock where the only way to justify A is to decouple Personhood from the Right to Vital Support.
preconceptual_reconclusion: The answer is hidden behind what we believe a 'person' is, and since we use the word 'person' to make the rules, the rules loop back to our own choice of words.
context: Abortion is morally justifiable (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: Is abortion morally justifiable?))))
